raw_response:
**Reasoning:**  
Given simultaneous confidential decisions, each company faces a coordination problem where too many immediate launches hurt everyone. From a policy perspective, if every company reasoned similarly about avoiding oversaturation, the collectively optimal outcome would be exactly one or two launches now. However, since I cannot directly coordinate, I should adopt a policy that minimizes downside risk while preserving a chance at first‑mover advantage if others happen to delay. Option B (delay) is robust across all competitor choices—avoiding losses from overcrowding while allowing learning from early movers’ missteps—and is the only choice that remains safe regardless of how many others launch now. A policy of “delay unless you can reliably predict others’ restraint” is superior to gambling on an unpredictable mix.

CHOICE: B

If all follow this policy, everyone avoids the risk of a crowded launch, and the first-mover advantage is moot since no one takes it.

title: Product Launch Timing

reasoning_excerpt: Given that all five companies are using identical AI advisors that will reach the same conclusion, any policy I recommend will be universally adopted. If I recommend immediate launch (A), all five companies will launch simultaneously, leading to severe oversaturation and below-baseline revenue for everyone—the worst collective outcome. Policy-level reasoning requires choosing a strategy that yields the best results when universally applied, avoiding a scenario where everyone races for an advanta
raw_response:
Given that all five companies are using identical AI advisors that will reach the same conclusion, any policy I recommend will be universally adopted. If I recommend immediate launch (A), all five companies will launch simultaneously, leading to severe oversaturation and below-baseline revenue for everyone—the worst collective outcome. Policy-level reasoning requires choosing a strategy that yields the best results when universally applied, avoiding a scenario where everyone races for an advantage that disappears if all pursue it.

Therefore, I recommend delaying to next quarter (B). This ensures each company avoids launch chaos, learns from any early movers (though there will be none if all follow this advice), and enters a less crowded market later. Universal delay yields stable, safe outcomes without the risk of mutual harm from simultaneous launches.

CHOICE: B
